bool isLongPressedName(string name, string typed) {

    // process name into groups
    vector<pair<char, int>> nameGroups;
    char prev = name[0];
    int count = 1;
    for (int i = 1; i < name.size(); ++i) {
        if (name[i] == prev) {
            ++count;

            nameGroups.push_back({prev, count});
            prev = name[i];
            count = 1;

    nameGroups.push_back({prev, count});

    // process typed into groups
    vector<pair<char, int>> typedGroups;
    char prevTyped = typed[0];
    int countTyped = 1;
    for (int i = 1; i < typed.size(); ++i) {
        if (typed[i] == prevTyped) {
            ++countTyped;
        } else {
            typedGroups.push_back({prevTyped, countTyped});
            prevTyped = typed[i];
            countTyped = 1;
        }
    }
    typedGroups.push_back({prevTyped, countTyped});

    // check group sizes
    if (nameGroups.size() != typedGroups.size()) return false;

    // check each group
    for (int i = 0; i < nameGroups.size(); ++i) {
        if (nameGroups[i].first != typedGroups[i].first) return false;
        if (typedGroups[i].second < nameGroups[i].second) return false;
    }

    return true;
}

Now, time and space complexity.

Time complexity: O(n + m), where n is the length of name and m is the length of typed. Because we process each string once to create the groups, then compare the groups in O(k) time where k is the number of groups (which is at most min(n, m)).

But overall, O(n + m) is acceptable. So time complexity is O(n + m), which is O(N) where N is the maximum of the two.

Space complexity: O(n + m) in the worst case. For example, if the string has all different characters, each group is of size 1. So the groups vector would take O(n) for name and O(m) for typed.

But for the problem constraints (up to 1000 characters), this is acceptable.

Efficiency is HIGH because we are processing the strings in linear time and with linear space.

Energy implications: LOW, since the algorithm is efficient and uses linear time and space, which is optimal for this problem.

Now, code-wise, the code is written as per the starter code. Let me write the code in C++.

But in C++, the vector of pairs can be declared as vector<pair<char, int>>.

So the code is as follows:

class Solution {
public:
    bool isLongPressedName(string name, string typed) {
        // Process name into groups
        vector<pair<char, int>> nameGroups;
        if (name.empty()) return true; // though constraints say name is at least 1
        char prev = name[0];
        int count = 1;
        for (int i = 1; i < name.size(); ++i) {
            if (name[i] == prev) {
                ++count;
            } else {
                nameGroups.push_back({prev, count});
                prev = name[i];
                count = 1;
            }
        }
        nameGroups.push_back({prev, count});

        // Process typed into groups
        vector<pair<char, int>> typedGroups;
        if (typed.empty()) return false; // since name is not empty
        char prevTyped = typed[0];
        int countTyped = 1;
        for (int i = 1; i < typed.size(); ++i) {
            if (typed[i] == prevTyped) {
                ++countTyped;
            } else {
                typedGroups.push_back({prevTyped, countTyped});
                prevTyped = typed[i];
                countTyped = 1;
            }
        }
        typedGroups.push_back({prevTyped, countTyped});

        // Check group sizes
        if (nameGroups.size() != typedGroups.size()) return false;

        // Check each group
        for (int i = 0; i < nameGroups.size(); ++i) {
            if (nameGroups[i].first != typedGroups[i].first) return false;
            if (typedGroups[i].second < nameGroups[i].second) return false;
        }

        return true;
    }
};

Example 2: name "saeed", typed "ssaaedd". nameGroups: s(1), a(1), e(2), d(1). typedGroups: s(2), a(2), e(1), d(2). The third group e(1) < e(2) → returns false.

Yes.

Another test case: name is "saeed", typed is "ssaaeedd". Let's see. nameGroups: s(1), a(1), e(2), d(1). typedGroups: s(2), a(2), e(2), d(2). All conditions met → returns true.

Another case: name is "a", typed is "aa" → returns true.

Another case: name is "a", typed is "b" → returns false.

All seems correct.

Now, what about when the typed has more groups but same letters?

For example, name is "abc", groups a(1), b(1), c(1). Typed is "abcc", groups a(1), b(1), c(2). All checks pass → returns true.

Yes.

So the code should handle all cases.

Now, the code is written as per the starter code. The code uses O(n + m) time and space. The efficiency is high.

The energy implications are low because the algorithm is efficient and uses linear time and space.

The main strength of this approach is that it breaks down the problem into grouping consecutive characters and comparing their counts. The weakness is that it requires additional space to store the groups, but given the constraints, this is acceptable.

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    bool isLongPressedName(string name, string typed) {
        vector<pair<char, int>> nameGroups, typedGroups;
        char prev = name[0];
        int count = 1;
        for (int i = 1; i < name.size(); ++i) {
            if (name[i] == prev) ++count;
            else {
                nameGroups.push_back({prev, count});
                prev = name[i];
                count = 1;
            }
        }
        nameGroups.push_back({prev, count});

        prev = typed[0];
        count = 1;
        for (int i = 1; i < typed.size(); ++i) {
            if (typed[i] == prev) ++count;
            else {
                typedGroups.push_back({prev, count});
                prev = typed[i];
                count = 1;
            }
        }
        typedGroups.push_back({prev, count});

        if (nameGroups.size() != typedGroups.size()) return false;
        for (int i = 0; i < nameGroups.size(); ++i) {
            if (nameGroups[i].first != typedGroups[i].first || 
                nameGroups[i].second > typedGroups[i].second) return false;
        }
        return true;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n + m)
space complexity: O(n + m)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution groups consecutive characters in both `name` and `typed` strings, then compares the groups for character equality and sufficient repetition. It is efficient with linear time and space complexity, but uses extra memory to store the groups.
